revision.comment: /* 明治末期の神社合祀 */
revision.text: '''神社合祀'''（じんじゃごうし）とは、[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の合併[[Policy|政策]]のことである。'''神社整理'''ともいう。複数の神社の[[Saijin|祭神]]を一つの神社に合祀 ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}) させるか、もしくは一つの神社の[[Setsumatsusha|摂末社]]にまとめて[[:de:Sengū|遷座]]させ、その他の神社を廃することによって、神社の数を減らすというもの。主に[[Meiji (era)|明治時代]]末期に行われたものをさす。 '''A shrine ritual''' is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the Setsumatsusha of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine enshrinement at the beginning of the Meiji era == == Shrine enshrinement at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of shrine enshrinement === The purpose of shrine enshrinement was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle (sect Shinto|denominational Shinto) that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to [[Large Shrine|prefectural shrine]]s and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[:Ministry of the Interior_(Japan)|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the [[:ja:内務省_(日本)|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[[:ja:神社局|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's [[:ja:地方局|Regional Bureau]] was also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of shrine enshrinement, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[:ja:財政|財政|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == 合祀された神社の復祀 == 戦後になると、戦前の神社非宗教体制は解体され、すべてが[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]となった。一度合祀されたもののちに復祀された神社も少なくなかった。名目上合祀された後も、社殿などの設備を残したところもあり、そういったところでは復祀が行なわれ易かった。全般的にみて、合祀以前の崇敬基盤がその後も維持されたところでは復祀が行なわれ易かったが、行政区画の統廃合や状況の変化で崇敬基盤となった共同体が消滅や変化をした場合は復祀されない傾向にあった。 === 神社合祀の禍根と神社神道の分裂 === 戦後の復祀の動きと共に、かつての強制的な合祀の政策に対する反感が業界内で表面化した。[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では官国幣社主導の[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]加盟の動きに反発する形で神社本教が分離独立、[[Hokkaido|北海道]]では{{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}が神社本庁と決別する形で設立された。 == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

revision.comment: /* Shrine enshrinement at the end of the Meiji period */
revision.text: '''神社合祀'''（じんじゃごうし）とは、[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の合併[[Policy|政策]]のことである。'''神社整理'''ともいう。複数の神社の[[Saijin|祭神]]を一つの神社に合祀 ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}) させるか、もしくは一つの神社の[[Setsumatsusha|摂末社]]にまとめて[[:de:Sengū|遷座]]させ、その他の神社を廃することによって、神社の数を減らすというもの。主に[[Meiji (era)|明治時代]]末期に行われたものをさす。 '''A shrine ritual''' is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the Setsumatsusha of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine enshrinement at the beginning of the Meiji era == == Shrine enshrinement at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of shrine enshrinement === The purpose of shrine enshrinement was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle (sect Shinto|denominational Shinto) that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to [[Large Shrine|prefectural shrine]]s and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[:Ministry of the Interior_(Japan)|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the [[:ja:内務省_(日本)|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[:ja:神社局|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's [[:ja:地方局|Regional Bureau]] was also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of shrine enshrinement, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[:ja:財政|財政|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == 合祀された神社の復祀 == 戦後になると、戦前の神社非宗教体制は解体され、すべてが[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]となった。一度合祀されたもののちに復祀された神社も少なくなかった。名目上合祀された後も、社殿などの設備を残したところもあり、そういったところでは復祀が行なわれ易かった。全般的にみて、合祀以前の崇敬基盤がその後も維持されたところでは復祀が行なわれ易かったが、行政区画の統廃合や状況の変化で崇敬基盤となった共同体が消滅や変化をした場合は復祀されない傾向にあった。 === 神社合祀の禍根と神社神道の分裂 === 戦後の復祀の動きと共に、かつての強制的な合祀の政策に対する反感が業界内で表面化した。[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では官国幣社主導の[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]加盟の動きに反発する形で神社本教が分離独立、[[Hokkaido|北海道]]では{{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}が神社本庁と決別する形で設立された。 == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == 合祀された神社の復祀 == 戦後になると、戦前の神社非宗教体制は解体され、すべてが[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]となった。一度合祀されたもののちに復祀された神社も少なくなかった。名目上合祀された後も、社殿などの設備を残したところもあり、そういったところでは復祀が行なわれ易かった。全般的にみて、合祀以前の崇敬基盤がその後も維持されたところでは復祀が行なわれ易かったが、行政区画の統廃合や状況の変化で崇敬基盤となった共同体が消滅や変化をした場合は復祀されない傾向にあった。 === 神社合祀の禍根と神社神道の分裂 === 戦後の復祀の動きと共に、かつての強制的な合祀の政策に対する反感が業界内で表面化した。[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では官国幣社主導の[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]加盟の動きに反発する形で神社本教が分離独立、[[Hokkaido|北海道]]では{{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}が神社本庁と決別する形で設立された。 == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

）とは、[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の合併[[Policy|政策]]のことである。'''神社整理'''ともいう。複数の神社の[[Saijin|祭神]]を一つの神社に合祀 ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}) させるか、もしくは一つの神社の[[Setsumatsusha|摂末社]]にまとめて[[:de:Sengū|遷座]]させ、その他の神社を廃することによって、神社の数を減らすというもの。主に[[Meiji (era)|明治時代]]末期に行われたものをさす。 {{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the Setsumatsusha of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine enshrinement at the beginning of the Meiji era == == Shrine enshrinement at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of shrine enshrinement === The purpose of shrine enshrinement was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle (sect Shinto|denominational Shinto) that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省_(日本)}} {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of shrine enshrinement, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == 合祀された神社の復祀 == 戦後になると、戦前の神社非宗教体制は解体され、すべてが[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]となった。一度合祀されたもののちに復祀された神社も少なくなかった。名目上合祀された後も、社殿などの設備を残したところもあり、そういったところでは復祀が行なわれ易かった。全般的にみて、合祀以前の崇敬基盤がその後も維持されたところでは復祀が行なわれ易かったが、行政区画の統廃合や状況の変化で崇敬基盤となった共同体が消滅や変化をした場合は復祀されない傾向にあった。 === 神社合祀の禍根と神社神道の分裂 === 戦後の復祀の動きと共に、かつての強制的な合祀の政策に対する反感が業界内で表面化した。[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では官国幣社主導の[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]加盟の動きに反発する形で神社本教が分離独立、[[Hokkaido|北海道]]では{{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}が神社本庁と決別する形で設立された。 == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto [ edit | edit source ] === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

revision.comment: /* Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined */
revision.text: ）とは、[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の合併[[Policy|政策]]のことである。'''神社整理'''ともいう。複数の神社の[[Saijin|祭神]]を一つの神社に合祀 ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}) させるか、もしくは一つの神社の[[Setsumatsusha|摂末社]]にまとめて[[:de:Sengū|遷座]]させ、その他の神社を廃することによって、神社の数を減らすというもの。主に[[Meiji (era)|明治時代]]末期に行われたものをさす。 {{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the Setsumatsusha of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine enshrinement at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of shrine enshrinement === The purpose of shrine enshrinement was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省_(日本)}} {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of shrine enshrinement, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''合祀政策の経緯''' == 神社合祀政策は[[1906|1906年]]（[[Meiji (era)|明治]]39年）の第1次西園寺内閣において、内務大臣・原敬によって出された[[Edict|勅令]]によって進められ、当初は地域の実情に合わせかなりの幅を持たせたものであった。だが、第2次桂内閣の内務大臣[[Hirata Tosuke|平田東助]]がこの訓令を強固に推し進めることを厳命したため、全国で[[1914|1914年]]（[[Taishō|大正]]3年）までに約20万社あった神社の7万社が取り壊された。特に合祀政策が甚だしかったのは[[Mie Prefecture|三重県]]で、県下全神社のおよそ9割が廃されることとなった。[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]]や[[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]]もそれについで合祀政策が進められた。しかし、この政策を進めるのは知事の裁量に任されたため、その実行の程度は地域差が出るものとなり、[[Kyoto Prefecture|京都府]]では1割程度ですんだ。 この官僚的合理主義に基づいた神社合祀政策は、必ずしも氏子崇敬者の意に即して行なわれなかった。当然のことながら、生活集落と行政区画は一致するとは限らず、ところによっては合祀で氏神が居住地からはほど遠い場所に移されて、氏子が氏神参拝に行くことができなくなった地域もある。合祀を拒んだ神社もあったが、所によってはなかば強制的に合祀が行なわれた。 === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

revision.text: 複数の神社の[[Saijin|祭神]]を一つの神社に合祀 ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}) させるか、もしくは一つの神社の[[Setsumatsusha|摂末社]]にまとめて[[:de:Sengū|遷座]]させ、その他の神社を廃することによって、神社の数を減らすというもの。 {{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the Setsumatsusha of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省_(日本)}} {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''Background of the ritual policy''' == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%. This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed. === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Draft:Shrine Merger Movement]] to [[Shrine Merger Movement]]
revision.text: {{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]] == Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period == === Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system) [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究　第３巻第１号（2006 年６月））The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]]. This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省_(日本)}} {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy. The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities. == '''Background of the ritual policy''' == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%. This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed. === Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc. That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=2021年12月29日}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=June 2022}} [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

In the south, the shrine merger movement was # weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed. {{Citation needed span|These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.|date=June 2022}}However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.[''[[Wikipedia:Citation needed|要出典]]'']。 [[:Category:出典を必要とする記述のある記事/2021年12月]] == Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge. === The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] == References == == External Links == * [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年 [[:Category:State Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[:Category:Meiji period]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines]] [[:Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Drafts in foreign languages]]

revision.comment: clean up tagging and formatting
revision.text:
{{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata"> [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第３巻第１号（2006 年６月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省_(日本)}} {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{cn}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{cn}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
== References == <references />
== External Links ==
* [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年
[[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Meiji period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] ** {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

revision.text:
{{copyedit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The Shrine Merger Movement|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] merger [[policy]] . Also called '''shrine organization .''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. thing. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata"> [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] {{ill|Shinto shrine bureau|lt=Shrine Bureau|ja|神社局}}, but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==

=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata"> [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
