{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copyedit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ( {{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}} ), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata"> [http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す]鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ( [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39) , and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ( [[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]] , the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining , and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata , a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]] , strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{cn|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]] , the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]] , the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
== References == <references />
== External Links ==
* [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義]森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年
[[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Meiji period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

It mainly refers to those built at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]

This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the order be strongly implemented. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions did not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to which the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Devotees at some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.

# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in irreparable damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be act of revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合|じんじゃごうし}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.''' It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

== External links ==

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合祀|Jinja gōshi}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.'''<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合祀|Jinja gōshi}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and the start of the [[Taishō era|Taisho eras]], with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name=":0" />The [[Shrine Bureau]] in the [[Home Ministry]] planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name=":0" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The edict applied only to a carefully selected few shrines, and the government sought to stipend only those that were thriving. <ref name=":0" />The [[Eminent domain|surrender of land]] belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation, which allowed for easier implementation of shrine mergers. <ref name=":0" />Local authorities in [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Mie Prefecture|Mie prefecture]]<nowiki/>s in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name=":0" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the Shrine Bureau. <ref name=":0" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合祀|Jinja gōshi}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> It is said that the number of shrines will be reduced by enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or by relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines. It mainly refers to those performed at the end of the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji era .]]
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and the start of the [[Taishō era|Taisho eras]], with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name=":0" />The [[Shrine Bureau]] in the [[Home Ministry]] planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name=":0" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name=":0" />The [[Eminent domain|surrender of land]] belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name=":0" />Local authorities in [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Mie Prefecture|Mie prefecture]]<nowiki/>s in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name=":0" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the Shrine Bureau. <ref name=":0" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar shrine non-religious system was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.

However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of the shrine that was enshrined == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合祀|Jinja gōshi}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
It involved either enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and the start of the [[Taishō era|Taisho eras]], with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name=":0" />The [[Shrine Bureau]] in the [[Home Ministry]] planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name=":0" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name=":0" />The [[Eminent domain|surrender of land]] belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name=":0" />Local authorities in [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Mie Prefecture|Mie prefecture]]<nowiki/>s in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name=":0" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the Shrine Bureau. <ref name=":0" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once enshrined and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances. It tended not to be revenge.
=== The sword of the shrine ritual and the division of the shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war revenge movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy surfaced within the industry. [[Kyoto Prefecture|In Kyoto Prefecture]], the Jinja Honkyo was spun off in response to the movement to join the Association of [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Shrines]] led by the Government of Japan, and in [[Hokkaido]], the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} was established in a way that broke up with the Association of Shinto Shrines.

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Nihongo|The '''Shrine Merger Policy'''|神社合祀|Jinja gōshi}} is a historical policy of the [[Empire of Japan]] which sought to merge shrines'''.'''<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
It involved either enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and the start of the [[Taishō era|Taisho eras]], with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name=":0" />The [[Shrine Bureau]] in the [[Home Ministry]] planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name=":0" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name=":0" />The [[Eminent domain|surrender of land]] belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name=":0" />Local authorities in [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Mie Prefecture|Mie prefecture]]<nowiki/>s in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name=":0" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name=":0" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the [[Government of Meiji Japan]] to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines. In 1900, the Shrine Bureau (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of [[World War II]], although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|publisher=ProQuest|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Authority control}}

# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==
* [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義] 森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年

revision.comment: Rescuing 2 sources and tagging 0 as dead.) #IABot (v2.0.9.3
revision.text:
{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{copy edit|date=June 2022}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the [[Government of Meiji Japan]] to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="kokugakuin" /> In 1900, the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]] (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2021-08-01 |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy – 南方熊楠記念館 |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=web.archive.org |archive-date=1 August 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210801114742/http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of [[World War II]], although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|publisher=ProQuest|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}</ref>
[[Category:Meiji period]] [[Category:Politics of the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]]
==Overview== The process of Shrine Consolidation involved either enshrining the [[Saijin|deities]] of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Ill|Inahachi Kinten Shrine|ja|稲八金天神社}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the [[Meiji era|Meiji]] and the start of the [[Taishō era|Taisho eras]], with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name=":0" />The [[Shrine Bureau]] in the [[Home Ministry]] planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name=":0" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name=":0" />The [[Eminent domain|surrender of land]] belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name=":0" />Local authorities in [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Mie Prefecture|Mie prefecture]]<nowiki/>s in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name=":0" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150110032843/http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf |date=10 January 2015 }} 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>）The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for [[Administrative divisions of Japan|local governments]] to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of the Interior]].
This policy was led by the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]] [[Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional [[Public finance|fiscal]] burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of enshrinement. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a [[Towns of Japan|town]] [[Village (Japan)|village]] the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the [[edict]] issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in [[1906]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. there were. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by [[1914]] ([[Taishō|Taisho]] 3), were demolished nationwide because [[Hirata Tosuke|Tosuke Hirata]], the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. rice field. [[Mie Prefecture|In particular, Mie Prefecture]] had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. [[Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama]] and [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime prefectures]] were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in [[Kyoto Prefecture]] it is about 10%.
This shrine ritual policy based on bureaucratic rationalism was not always carried out in the will of the worshipers of the parishioners. As a matter of course, the settlements and administrative divisions do not always match, and in some areas, the [[Ujigami]] was moved to a place far from the place of residence due to the goshi, and the [[Ujigami]] could not go to worship. Some shrines refused to worship, but in some places the worship was forcibly performed.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.
That said, intellectuals such as [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata, a]] [[Natural history|naturalist]] and [[Folklore studies|folklore]] scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy . In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in enshrinement was temporarily halted after [[1910]] (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of enshrinement had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}} However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.{{citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
== See also ==
* [[Shinto]] ** [[State Shinto]] ** [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
== References == <references />
== External links ==
* [http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000093/card525.html 南方熊楠「神社合祀に関する意見」]（[[Aozora Bunko|青空文庫]]） * [http://www.seijo.ac.jp/graduate/gslit/orig/journal/jomin/pdf/sjpn-02-01.pdf 神社をめぐる住民感情と官僚的合理主義] 森岡清美、『日本常民文化紀要』、1975年
[[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Meiji period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] {{State Shinto}} {{Shinto shrine}}{{Authority control}}
