revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]]
{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2023}} {{Original research|date=June 2023}} }} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="kokugakuin" /> In 1900, the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]] (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a">{{Cite web |date=2021-08-01 |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy – 南方熊楠記念館 |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-date=1 August 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210801114742/http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}{{Dead link|date=December 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
==Overview== The process of Shrine Consolidation involved either enshrining the deities of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Section link||Inahachikonten Shrine}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} and the start of the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho eras|1=Taishō era}}, with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The [[:en:Shrine Bureau|Shrine Bureau]] in the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}} planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The {{ill|Eminent domain|en|Eminent domain|WD=Q166332|lt=surrender of land|1=Eminent domain}} belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />Local authorities in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie prefectures|1=Mie Prefecture}} in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150110032843/http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf |date=10 January 2015 }} 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>. The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|WD=Q1850442|lt=local governments|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}} to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of the Interior|1=Home Ministry}}.
This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} [[:en:Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|1=Regional Bureaus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424372}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional fiscal burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of Shrine merging. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a {{ill|Towns of Japan|en|Towns of Japan|WD=Q1059478|lt=town|1=Towns of Japan}} {{ill|Village (Japan)|en|Village (Japan)|WD=Q4174776|lt=village|1=Village (Japan)}} the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=Edict|1=Edict}} issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in {{ill|1906|en|1906|WD=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by {{ill|1914|en|1914|WD=Q2083|lt=1914|1=1914}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho|1=Taishō era}} 3), were demolished nationwide because {{ill|Hirata Tosuke|en|Hirata Tosuke|WD=Q3136218|lt=Tosuke Hirata|1=Hirata Tosuke}}, the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=In particular, Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime prefectures|1=Ehime Prefecture}} were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} it is about 10%.
The shrine consolidation policy, which was based on bureaucratic rationalism, was not always implemented in accordance with the wishes of the shrine parishioners and worshippers. Settlements and administrative divisions did not always match, which resulted in some [[Ujigami]] being moved to places far from their worshippers due to the shrine mergers. As a result, some [[Ujigami]] were unable to be worshipped. Although some shrine parishioners refused to worship, in some places they were forced to worship.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-31 |website=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref>
That said, intellectuals such as {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|WD=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata, a|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} {{ill|Natural history|en|Natural history|WD=Q484591|lt=naturalist|1=Natural history}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore|1=Folklore studies}} scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/kumagusu/life/anti-shrine-consolidation-protests |access-date=2023-04-05 |website=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref> In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[:en:Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in Shrine merging was temporarily halted after {{ill|1910|en|1910|WD=Q2075|lt=1910|1=1910}} (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of Shrine merging had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs. However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.
== Inahachikonten Shrine == Inahachikonten Shrine (稲八金天神社) is an alleged shrine name of many such merged shrines in the {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}.
The name was intended as a merger of the first characters of the most frequently merged shrines {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrines]], and [[Tenmangū|Tenmangu Shrine]].
{{ill|Masajiro Takigawa|ja|瀧川政次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masajiro Takigawa|1=Masajirō Takikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566311}} said such shrines lacked sacredness
No such shrines have been confirmed to have this name, possibly because they changed their names in embarrassment.
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
== See also ==

{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2023}} {{Original research|date=June 2023}} }} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="kokugakuin" /> In 1900, the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]] (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a">{{Cite web |date=2021-08-01 |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy – 南方熊楠記念館 |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-date=1 August 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210801114742/http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}{{Dead link|date=December 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
==Overview== The process of Shrine Consolidation involved either enshrining the deities of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Section link||Inahachikonten Shrine}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} and the start of the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho eras|1=Taishō era}}, with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The [[:en:Shrine Bureau|Shrine Bureau]] in the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}} planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The {{ill|Eminent domain|en|Eminent domain|WD=Q166332|lt=surrender of land|1=Eminent domain}} belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />Local authorities in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie prefectures|1=Mie Prefecture}} in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150110032843/http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf |date=10 January 2015 }} 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>. The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|WD=Q1850442|lt=local governments|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}} to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of the Interior|1=Home Ministry}}.
This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} [[:en:Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|1=Regional Bureaus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424372}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional fiscal burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of Shrine merging. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a {{ill|Towns of Japan|en|Towns of Japan|WD=Q1059478|lt=town|1=Towns of Japan}} {{ill|Village (Japan)|en|Village (Japan)|WD=Q4174776|lt=village|1=Village (Japan)}} the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=Edict|1=Edict}} issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in {{ill|1906|en|1906|WD=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by {{ill|1914|en|1914|WD=Q2083|lt=1914|1=1914}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho|1=Taishō era}} 3), were demolished nationwide because {{ill|Hirata Tosuke|en|Hirata Tosuke|WD=Q3136218|lt=Tosuke Hirata|1=Hirata Tosuke}}, the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=In particular, Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime prefectures|1=Ehime Prefecture}} were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} it is about 10%.
The shrine consolidation policy, which was based on bureaucratic rationalism, was not always implemented in accordance with the wishes of the shrine parishioners and worshippers. Settlements and administrative divisions did not always match, which resulted in some [[Ujigami]] being moved to places far from their worshippers due to the shrine mergers. As a result, some [[Ujigami]] were unable to be worshipped. Although some shrine parishioners refused to worship, in some places they were forced to worship.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-31 |website=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref>
That said, intellectuals such as {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|WD=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata, a|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} {{ill|Natural history|en|Natural history|WD=Q484591|lt=naturalist|1=Natural history}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore|1=Folklore studies}} scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/kumagusu/life/anti-shrine-consolidation-protests |access-date=2023-04-05 |website=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref> In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[:en:Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in Shrine merging was temporarily halted after {{ill|1910|en|1910|WD=Q2075|lt=1910|1=1910}} (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of Shrine merging had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs. However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.
== Inahachikonten Shrine == Inahachikonten Shrine (稲八金天神社) is an alleged shrine name of many such merged shrines in the {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}.
The name was intended as a merger of the first characters of the most frequently merged shrines {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrines]], and [[Tenmangū|Tenmangu Shrine]].
{{ill|Masajiro Takigawa|ja|瀧川政次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masajiro Takigawa|1=Masajirō Takikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566311}} said such shrines lacked sacredness
No such shrines have been confirmed to have this name, possibly because they changed their names in embarrassment.
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]]
{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2023}} {{Original research|date=June 2023}} }} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="kokugakuin" /> In 1900, the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]] (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a">{{Cite web |date=2021-08-01 |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy – 南方熊楠記念館 |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-date=1 August 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210801114742/http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}{{Dead link|date=December 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
==Overview== The process of Shrine Consolidation involved either enshrining the deities of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Section link||Inahachikonten Shrine}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} and the start of the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho eras|1=Taishō era}}, with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The [[:en:Shrine Bureau|Shrine Bureau]] in the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}} planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The {{ill|Eminent domain|en|Eminent domain|WD=Q166332|lt=surrender of land|1=Eminent domain}} belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />Local authorities in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie prefectures|1=Mie Prefecture}} in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150110032843/http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf |date=10 January 2015 }} 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>. The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|WD=Q1850442|lt=local governments|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}} to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of the Interior|1=Home Ministry}}.
This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} [[:en:Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|1=Regional Bureaus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424372}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional fiscal burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of Shrine merging. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a {{ill|Towns of Japan|en|Towns of Japan|WD=Q1059478|lt=town|1=Towns of Japan}} {{ill|Village (Japan)|en|Village (Japan)|WD=Q4174776|lt=village|1=Village (Japan)}} the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=Edict|1=Edict}} issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in {{ill|1906|en|1906|WD=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by {{ill|1914|en|1914|WD=Q2083|lt=1914|1=1914}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho|1=Taishō era}} 3), were demolished nationwide because {{ill|Hirata Tosuke|en|Hirata Tosuke|WD=Q3136218|lt=Tosuke Hirata|1=Hirata Tosuke}}, the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=In particular, Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime prefectures|1=Ehime Prefecture}} were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} it is about 10%.
The shrine consolidation policy, which was based on bureaucratic rationalism, was not always implemented in accordance with the wishes of the shrine parishioners and worshippers. Settlements and administrative divisions did not always match, which resulted in some [[Ujigami]] being moved to places far from their worshippers due to the shrine mergers. As a result, some [[Ujigami]] were unable to be worshipped. Although some shrine parishioners refused to worship, in some places they were forced to worship.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-31 |website=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref>
That said, intellectuals such as {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|WD=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata, a|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} {{ill|Natural history|en|Natural history|WD=Q484591|lt=naturalist|1=Natural history}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore|1=Folklore studies}} scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/kumagusu/life/anti-shrine-consolidation-protests |access-date=2023-04-05 |website=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref> In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[:en:Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in Shrine merging was temporarily halted after {{ill|1910|en|1910|WD=Q2075|lt=1910|1=1910}} (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of Shrine merging had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs. However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.
== Inahachikonten Shrine == Inahachikonten Shrine (稲八金天神社) is an alleged shrine name of many such merged shrines in the {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}.
The name was intended as a merger of the first characters of the most frequently merged shrines {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrines]], and [[Tenmangū|Tenmangu Shrine]].
{{ill|Masajiro Takigawa|ja|瀧川政次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masajiro Takigawa|1=Masajirō Takikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566311}} said such shrines lacked sacredness
No such shrines have been confirmed to have this name, possibly because they changed their names in embarrassment.
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shrine Consolidation Policy]][[fr:Politique de consolidation des sanctuaires]][[ja:神社合祀]][[simple:Shrine Consolidation Policy]]
{{Short description|Empire of Japan policy to merge shrines}} {{Multiple issues| {{More citations needed|date=May 2023}} {{Original research|date=June 2023}} }} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} The '''Shrine Consolidation Policy''' ('''''Jinja seirei''''', also '''''Jinja gōshi''''', '''''Jinja gappei''''') was an effort by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} to abolish numerous smaller [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and consolidate their functions with larger regional shrines.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8856 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="kokugakuin" /> In 1900, the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]] (''Jinja kyoku'') was created as a branch of the Home Ministry, and it was this organ that was responsible for the implementation of the policy.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{cite web|last1=Koremaru|first1=Sakamoto|title=Jinja gōshi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1105|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|accessdate=13 March 2017}}</ref>
The aim of the policy was to reduce the political influence of Shinto, bringing the remaining shrines under government jurisdiction and making them easier to control. Within the first twenty years of the policy, 77,899 Shinto shrines were closed, despite considerable local opposition.<ref name="Picken2010">{{cite book|author=Stuart D. B. Picken|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA136|date=28 December 2010|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=136–137}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a">{{Cite web |date=2021-08-01 |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy – 南方熊楠記念館 |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-date=1 August 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210801114742/http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> The policy remained in effect until the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although its greatest impact occurred in the first six years after its implementation in 1906; by 1912, the rate at which shrines were closed had fallen considerably.<ref name="Todd2007">{{cite book|author=Christopher M. Todd|title=Mapping the Gods: A Geographic Analysis of the Effects of the Shrine Merger Policy on Japanese Sacred Space|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4pLrsRRxAdEC&pg=PA1|year=2007|isbn=978-0-549-42677-6|pages=1–2}}{{Dead link|date=December 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
==Overview== The process of Shrine Consolidation involved either enshrining the deities of multiple shrines in one shrine ({{Section link||Inahachikonten Shrine}}), or relocating them to the [[Setsumatsusha]] of one [[Setsumatsusha|shrine]] and eliminating the other shrines.
The Japanese government carried out shrine merger policies between the end of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} and the start of the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho eras|1=Taishō era}}, with the goal of enhancing the presence of shrines as a whole.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The [[:en:Shrine Bureau|Shrine Bureau]] in the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}} planned the reorganization and abolition of smaller, unviable shrines, particularly village shrines and unranked shrines. The government's stated purpose was to organize existing shrines and limit the provision of shrine offerings to a select few. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> However it was accused of corruption and attempting to seize land for financial gain {{Citation needed|date=March 2023}} An imperial edict in 1906 entitled "With Regard to the Provision of Offerings to Shrines of Prefectural Rank and Below" was the beginning of this. The sought to financially support shrines but only those that were thriving, so the government started merging shrines so only supported shrines would be present. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />The {{ill|Eminent domain|en|Eminent domain|WD=Q166332|lt=surrender of land|1=Eminent domain}} belonging to merged shrines, temples, and other sacred sites was required under the 1906 edict, without compensation. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />Local authorities in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie prefectures|1=Mie Prefecture}} in particular forced through a rigorous program of mergers, resulting in a significant reduction in the number of shrines by 1914. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" />
Some intellectuals and politicians resisted the merger policy, leading to its eventual eschewal by the [[Bureau of Shrines|Shrine Bureau]]. <ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /><ref name="opposition-to-shrine-merger-policy-2021a" />
== Shrine merger at the end of the Meiji period ==
=== Purpose of Shrine merger === The purpose of Shrine merger was to reduce the number of shrines and concentrate expenses on the remaining shrines so that they would be equipped with facilities and property above a certain standard, to maintain the dignity of the shrines, and to establish the continuous management of the shrines. In addition, the Meiji government's national principle that [[sect Shinto]] is recognized as a religion, but that shrines are not religions but "national religious ceremonies" (suzoku, seiji, sasai system)<ref name="kamata">[http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf 「日本的霊性」を問い直す] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150110032843/http://mitizane.ll.chiba-u.jp/metadb/up/ReCPAcoe/kamata31.pdf |date=10 January 2015 }} 鎌田東二、千葉大学公共研究 第3巻第1号（2006 年6月）</ref>. The policy was also to reduce the number of shrines until it was financially feasible for {{ill|Administrative divisions of Japan|en|Administrative divisions of Japan|WD=Q1850442|lt=local governments|1=Administrative divisions of Japan}} to provide public funds to prefectural shrines and smaller shrines in accordance with the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of the Interior|1=Home Ministry}}.
This policy was led by the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|WD=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} [[:en:Shinto shrine bureau|Shrine Bureau]], but apparently the Ministry's {{ill|Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|lt=Regional Bureaus|ja|地方局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Regional Bureaus of the Home Ministry|1=Regional Bureaus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424372}} were also involved. The local bureaus were reluctant to allow local governments to contribute public funds to shrines below the prefectural level, which was one of the purposes of Shrine merger, because it would require local governments to bear an additional fiscal burden. It was incorporated into the policy.
The '''shrine-centered theory''' refers to the idea that local autonomy should be centered on shrines, and this led to the application of the "one town, one village, one shrine" standard to the policy of Shrine merging. By aligning the shrine's [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] area with administrative divisions, the government sought to make the sole shrine of a {{ill|Towns of Japan|en|Towns of Japan|WD=Q1059478|lt=town|1=Towns of Japan}} {{ill|Village (Japan)|en|Village (Japan)|WD=Q4174776|lt=village|1=Village (Japan)}} the center of local activities.
== Background of the ritual policy == The shrine ritual policy was promoted by the {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=Edict|1=Edict}} issued by the Minister of the Interior, Takashi Hara, in the 1st Saionji Cabinet in {{ill|1906|en|1906|WD=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 39), and initially it was given a considerable range according to the actual situation of the region. However, 70,000 shrines, which were about 200,000 by {{ill|1914|en|1914|WD=Q2083|lt=1914|1=1914}} ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho|1=Taishō era}} 3), were demolished nationwide because {{ill|Hirata Tosuke|en|Hirata Tosuke|WD=Q3136218|lt=Tosuke Hirata|1=Hirata Tosuke}}, the Minister of Interior of the Second Katsura Cabinet, ordered that the instruction be strongly promoted. {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=In particular, Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} had a particularly strong policy of enshrining, and about 90% of all shrines in the prefecture were abolished. {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime prefectures|1=Ehime Prefecture}} were followed by a ritual policy. However, since it was left to the governor's discretion to proceed with this policy, the degree of implementation will vary from region to region, and in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} it is about 10%.
The shrine consolidation policy, which was based on bureaucratic rationalism, was not always implemented in accordance with the wishes of the shrine parishioners and worshippers. Settlements and administrative divisions did not always match, which resulted in some [[Ujigami]] being moved to places far from their worshippers due to the shrine mergers. As a result, some [[Ujigami]] were unable to be worshipped. Although some shrine parishioners refused to worship, in some places they were forced to worship.
=== Opposition movement === On the part of the shrine parishioners and worshippers, they sometimes held meetings in opposition, but mainly they could not make any major movements, and could only show their dissatisfaction in the form of talking about how the deities of the shrines that had been abolished by the merged shrine had caused hauntings, etc.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/exhibition-guide/corner4/corner4-2 |access-date=2023-03-31 |website=Opposition to Shrine Merger Policy｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref>
That said, intellectuals such as {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|WD=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata, a|1=Minakata Kumagusu}} {{ill|Natural history|en|Natural history|WD=Q484591|lt=naturalist|1=Natural history}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore|1=Folklore studies}} scholar known for his research on [[Myxogastria|slime molds]], strongly opposed this policy.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |url=http://www.minakatakumagusu-kinenkan.jp/english/kumagusu/life/anti-shrine-consolidation-protests |access-date=2023-04-05 |website=Anti-Shrine-Consolidation Protests｜Minakata Kumagusu Museum |language=en}}</ref> In the south, the shrine merger movement was
# weakening the godly thought, # hindering the harmony of the people, # debilitating the regions, # depriving the people of comfort, diminishing humanity and harming customs, # damaging patriotism, and # damaging security of the land. # historic sites and ancient traditions would be destroyed # natural landscapes and natural monuments would be destroyed.
These opposition movements gradually came to a halt, and through the responses of the [[:en:Imperial Diet of Japan|Imperial Diet]], the sudden increase in Shrine merging was temporarily halted after {{ill|1910|en|1910|WD=Q2075|lt=1910|1=1910}} (1910-1943). However, it was already too late, and this policy of Shrine merging had left a large impact, resulting in the disappearance of many rituals and customs, and damaging religious beliefs. However, it was too late, and the scars left by this ritual policy were large, and many ritual customs disappeared, resulting in damage to religious beliefs.
== Inahachikonten Shrine == Inahachikonten Shrine (稲八金天神社) is an alleged shrine name of many such merged shrines in the {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}.
The name was intended as a merger of the first characters of the most frequently merged shrines {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrines]], and [[Tenmangū|Tenmangu Shrine]].
{{ill|Masajiro Takigawa|ja|瀧川政次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masajiro Takigawa|1=Masajirō Takikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566311}} said such shrines lacked sacredness
No such shrines have been confirmed to have this name, possibly because they changed their names in embarrassment.
== Reconstruction of destroyed shrines == After the war, the prewar [[Secular Shrine Theory|shrine non-religious system]] was dismantled and everything became a [[religious corporation]] . Many shrines were once merged and then revived. Even after the shrine was nominally enshrined, there were some places where facilities such as the shrine were left, and it was easy to rehabilitate in those places. Overall, it was easy to rehabilitate where the pre-government reverence base was maintained, but if the community that became the worship base disappeared or changed due to the consolidation of administrative divisions or changes in circumstances.
=== Influence on the division of Shrine Shinto === Along with the post-war reconstruction movement, antipathy to the former compulsory goshi policy is seen as responsible for the splitting of the {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido shrine association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}} off from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
